segments.s238-g49: نشان is the direct noun 'a sign/trace' and here functions as the thing sought. خواست is the simple past (3rd person singular) of خواستن, 'he wanted/asked/ sought.' از introduces the source/agent 'from,' and شاه توران سپاه is a compact, poetic noun sequence meaning 'the army of the king of Turan' (شاه توران = 'the Turanian king'; سپاه = 'army'). Word order is terse/poetic: object ('نشان') + verb ('خواست') + prepositional source phrase.
segments.s238-g50: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' (ز = 'from' in more elevated/poetic register; هر سو = 'each side/direction'). بجستند is past tense 3rd person plural 'they searched/sought,' with its object (the previously mentioned نشان) understood. بی‌راه و راه is a paired contrast 'off the road and the road' (بی‌راه = 'without road/off-road' in this context, و = 'and', راه = 'road/path'), emphasizing searching both along paths and across pathless ground.
segments.s238-g51: نشانی is the subject 'a sign/trace.' نیامد is the past negative construction 'did not come' (literally 'came not'). ز افراسیاب is a source phrase 'from Afrasiab.' Together this clause states that no trace came from Afrasiab.
segments.s238-g52: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. Each نه is followed by a prepositional phrase with بر ('on/upon/at'): بر کوه و دریا = 'on the mountain and the sea,' and بر خشک و آب = 'on dry land and on water.' The repetition of بر and the paired nouns emphasizes there was no trace anywhere — on heights, on the sea, on land or on water.
segments.s238-g17: This is a simple past clause: subject سپه (the host/army) + adverbial سربسر (all around/everywhere) + object نعره (a shout) + verb برداشتند (they raised/took up). برداشتن in poetic narrative often means 'to lift/raise' here; برداشتند is 3rd person plural past.
segments.s238-g18: Subject همه (all/everyone) is followed by direct object نیزه (spears) and the prepositional phrase بر کوه (on/onto the hill), with the verb بگذاشتند (they placed) at the end in the past tense. Word order (subject — object — location — verb) is typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s238-g19: چنان شد is an intransitive past construction meaning 'it became so' or 'thus it happened.' The noun phrase در و دشت ('dar and dasht') are coordinated nouns (here 'gate/court' and 'plain'), and آوردگاه is the predicate noun 'battlefield' that completes the copular idea introduced by شد.
segments.s238-g20: که introduces a result/explanatory clause: از کشته ('on account of/from the slain') is a prepositional phrase giving the cause, while جایی ندیدند راه literally places 'a place' (جایی) before the negative past verb ندیدند ('they did not see'), with راه ('a way/path') as the notion they failed to find — idiomatically 'they saw no way anywhere.'
segments.s238-g21: برفتند is the past 3rd plural verb 'they went/left.' یک بهره ('one portion/one party') specifies a group; زنهار خواه is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning 'seeking zinehâr' — i.e., pleading or asking for mercy/warning — where خواه is the verbal root 'to want/ask' used attributively. The whole means 'one group left asking for mercy.'
segments.s238-g22: گریزان is a present participle/adjective 'fleeing' modifying the understood subject; برفتند repeats the past verb 'they went/left.' بهری براه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'toward the road' or 'on the road' (بهر/بهری = to/toward, براه = on the road), giving direction.
segments.s238-g23: شد is the past copula 'became' or 'happened.' از بی‌شبانی ('because of/from lack of night-watch') gives the reason. The nouns رمه تال و مال are a coordinated list of affected items (rheme = flock/herd; تال here as low plain/valley; مال = possessions/livestock). The phrase says these things were ruined/affected as a result.
segments.s238-g24: همه quantifies 'the whole/plain' (همه دشت = the entire plain). تن بود is a predicative construction: 'was a mass of bodies' (تن = body, بود = was). The final phrase بی‌دست و یال ('without hands and mane') is a/descriptive adjective phrase attached to that state — a grim image meaning corpses stripped of limbs and manes.
segments.s238-g33: زمانی چو (temporal + conjunction) sets a time clause: 'when/whenever'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آید is the present/poetic third-person singular 'comes'. بجنگ is an infinitival/purpose complement 'to fight' placed after the finite verb (آید بجنگ = 'comes to fight'). The phrase as a whole means 'when Ahriman comes to fight.'
segments.s238-g34: زمانی at the start of the segment again marks a contrasting time ('at another time' / 'sometimes'). عروسی is the noun 'wedding/feast'. پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر + از + noun). بوی و رنگ are the nouns 'scent' and 'color' joined by و. The clause means 'at another time (there is) a wedding full of scent and colour.'
segments.s238-g35: This is an exhortative clause. بی‌آزاری functions as a substantive ('harmlessness' / 'benign behavior') coordinated with جام by و. می‌برگزین is a present-form verb 'chooses/selects' used here as advising 'choose' (poetic exhortation uses present forms). The sense: 'Choose harmlessness and the cup.'
segments.s238-g36: که introduces a reason/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). گوید is the reporting verb 'he says' (poetic present). The second که is the embedded complementizer 'that' introducing reported speech. نفرین به از آفرین is an archaic comparative formula meaning 'a curse is preferable/better than praise' (به از = 'better than'). The whole clause: 'for he says that a curse is better than praise.'
segments.s238-g37: بخور is a second-person imperative 'eat'. آنچ داری is a relative/nominal phrase 'that which you have' (آنچ = 'what/that which'; داری = 'you have'). و links to انده مخور, where مخور is the negative imperative 'do not' and انده (archaic) means 'sorrow/grief' (so پارalleled as 'do not grieve'). The line advises: 'Eat what you have, and do not be sorrowful.'
segments.s238-g38: که introduces the reason ('for' / 'because'). گیتی سپنج است is a metaphorical predication 'the world is a sponge' (سپنج = sponge). و coordinates the next clause; ما بر گذر literally 'we on the passage' = 'we are passing by/are transient'. Together: 'for the world is a sponge and we are passing over it' (explaining why one should not grieve).
segments.s238-g39: میازار is the negative imperative 'do not harm / do not trouble'. کس را is the object 'anyone' with the accusative/topic marker را. ز بهر درم is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of a dirham' (ز بهر = 'for, on account of'; درم = a small coin). The clause warns: 'Do not harm anyone for the sake of a coin.'
segments.s238-g40: مکن is another negative imperative 'do not'. تا توانی here means 'as long as you are able' / 'as far as you can' (تا + ability verb gives a limitive sense). بکس is 'another person/one' (here 'anyone else'), and بر ستم means 'in/on oppression' i.e. 'to oppress.' The clause reads idiomatically: 'Do not, insofar as you can, oppress anyone.'
segments.s238-g9: سپاه (subject) + اندر آمد (verb) + بپیش سپاه (directional phrase). 'اندر آمد' is a simple past narrative ('came/advanced in'), with 'اندر' a poetic preposition meaning 'into/among'. 'بپیش' is a directional preposition 'toward/forward to' and links the verb to the second 'سپاه' (the opposing army). Word order is subject — verb — directional phrase.
segments.s238-g10: زمین گشت is a predicative clause: 'زمین' (the earth/ground) + 'گشت' (became/turned). The following phrase 'برسان ابر سیاه' is a poetic comparative: here 'برسان' functions like 'به‌سان / همچون' (i.e. 'like, as') introducing the image 'a black cloud'. Thus: 'the earth became (as) a black cloud.' Note: 'برسان' is an archaic/poetic usage here (comparative), not the ordinary verb 'to deliver'.
segments.s238-g11: تهمتن is the subject (a heroic name/epithet). 'به آواز گفت' is a fixed phrase: 'به آواز' = 'in a voice' → 'aloud', and 'گفت' is the past verb 'said'. 'آن زمان' is a temporal adverbial ('at that time') placed after the verb for emphasis: literally 'Tahmatan aloud said at that time.' Note: the dictionary senses provided for 'گفت' were nominal; here it is the past verb 'to say'.
segments.s238-g12: This is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' (= 'that/when/which'). 'نیزه مدارید' is an exhortative/imperative expression addressed to the troops—here meaning roughly 'brandish/prepare the spears' (an archaic verbal form; the exact lexical sense isn't in the supplied senses). The coordination 'و تیر و کمان' simply lists 'arrows and bow' (تیر = arrow, کمان = bow). Word order places the verb after its object for rhetorical force.
segments.s238-g13: بکوشید is a second-person-plural exhortative (imperative/subjunctive) meaning 'make an effort/strive' or 'do your best'. The conjunction 'و' links commands. 'شمشیر و گرز آورید' uses the plural imperative 'آورید' = 'bring (you all)', with a coordinated object 'swords and maces'. The verb repeated later continues the command.
segments.s238-g14: This is a continuation/clarification of the previous command. 'هنرها' (the skills/arts) is the object, 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from', 'بالای برز' is a doubled locative/emphatic phrase meaning 'from on high / from the lofty height' (بالای = atop, برز = loftiness/height), and 'آورید' repeats the imperative 'bring'. Word order is object — source — verb, typical in elevated/poetic diction.
segments.s238-g15: پلنگ is the subject. 'آن زمان' here is temporal ('then/at that time'). 'پیچد' is a narrative verb meaning 'twisted/turned' (an archaic/poetic past or narrative-form); 'از کین خویش' is an ablative-of-cause phrase: 'from/with its own kins' meaning 'out of its own rage/vengeance'. The clause describes an action prompted by fury. 'خویش' functions possessively ('its own') in an older register.
segments.s238-g16: 'که' introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that/so as to'). 'نخچیر بیند' uses the present/subjunctive 'بیند' (classical usage) to express purpose/result: 'that it may see/so that it sees its prey'. 'ببالین' is an adverbial compound meaning 'at its side / close beside', and 'خویش' again is the possessive 'its own' referring back to the panther. Overall the clause explains why it turned.
segments.s238-g41: بجست is a past-tense verb (3rd‑pl.) meaning “they searched/sought.” اندرران دشت is a prepositional/locative phrase: اندرران (therein/in that) + دشت (plain), so the clause reads literally “they searched therein the plain.” چیزی که بود is a relative clause (“something that was…”), with که introducing the relative and بود the past copula.
segments.s238-g42: ز here is the poetic preposition “of/from.” زرین modifies an implied material (“golden/of gold”), وز is an older poetic form in the line (here tied into the phrase linking two precious materials), گوهر is “gem/jewel,” and نابسود is an adjective qualifying those things (negative; here rendered “not wasted/unsquandered”). The phrase as a whole describes the stuff they sought (“of gold and jewels, not wasted”).
segments.s238-g43: سراسر is an adverb meaning “throughout/everywhere.” فرستاد is a past verb “he sent/ dispatched” (V follows adverb), and نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase “near the king” that indicates the destination/area affected by the sending.
segments.s238-g44: This is a simple enumerative noun phrase listing what was sent: غلامان “servants/pages,” اسپان “horses,” تیغ “swords,” and کلاه (here used as head‑gear) “helmets/hats.” Each و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” The list completes the object of فرستاد.
segments.s238-g45: وزان is a fused prepositional phrase (“and/from that” or “from that”), marking source; بهره خویشتن is a noun phrase “his share” with خویشتن emphatic/reflexive ‘himself/for himself.’ برگرفت is a past verb ‘he took (away)/removed’ — here it means he appropriated that share for himself.
segments.s238-g46: همه begins the following coordinated object “all the …” followed by a list: افسر (helmet/armour piece), مشک (musk), عنبر (ambergris/amber). گرفت is a past tense verb ‘took/seized’ completing the action already introduced by برگرفت; stylistically the two verbs together emphasize taking those goods.
segments.s238-g47: ببخشید is a past verb here meaning “he gave/bestowed” (not merely ‘forgave’). دیگر همه is a phrase meaning “the remaining/all the rest,” and بر سپاه (بر + سپاه) indicates the beneficiary/recipient “upon/to the army.” The verb distributes those remaining goods to the troops.
segments.s238-g48: ز is the preposition “from/of,” introducing the source phrase چیزی که بود اندران رزمگاه — literally “something that was therein the battlefield.” که introduces the relative clause modifying چیزی, بود is the past copula, and اندران (= اندر + ان) means “therein/among them,” locating the goods on the battlefield (رزمگاه).
segments.s238-g25: چنین گفت رستم که کشتن بسست — Word order is literary: چون/چنین (adverb) + گفت (verb) + رستم (subject) = "thus said Rostam." که introduces a subordinate clause. کشتن is the verbal noun (masdar) functioning as the subject of the subordinate clause, and بسست is an archaic fused form (بسّ است) meaning "is enough."
segments.s238-g26: که زهر زمان بهر دیگر کسست — که links/explains the preceding clause ("for/since/that"). زهر زمان is a compound genitive "poison/venom of Time" (figurative). بهر + دیگر کسست renders a dative/possessive idea: بهر = "for/to" and کسست = archaic "کس است" (= "is someone/belongs to someone"), so the phrase means "is for another person."
segments.s238-g27: زمانی همی بار زهر آورد — زمانی is an adverbial "at times/occasionally." همی is the archaic habitual/continual marker ("would/kept on"). بار here is "load/parcel," so بار زهر آورد literally "brought a load of poison," with آورد as simple past/preterite "he brought/used to bring."
segments.s238-g28: زمانی ز تریاک بهر آورد — again زمانی "at times." ز is the poetic preposition "from/of," marking source ("from opium"). بهر here functions as a dative/purpose marker "for/for the sake of," and آورد is the same past habitual verb "brought." The clause means "at other times he brought (poison) from opium."
segments.s238-g29: همه جامه رزم بیرون کنید — همه addresses all people (vocative/plural). جامه رزم is a genitive compound "garment of battle" = "war clothes/armour." بیرون کنید is an imperative (plural) formed with کردن + کنید meaning here "take out/put on/prepare" (i.e., equip yourselves for battle).
segments.s238-g30: همه خوبکاری بافزون کنید — همه again addresses everyone. خوبکاری is the object "good deed(s)/good conduct." بافزون is an intensifying/adverbial element (با-فزون = "increasingly/more") placed before the verb; کنید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative "do/increase." The clause means "All of you, increase your good deeds."
segments.s238-g31: چه بندی دل اندر سرای سپنج — چه is an interrogative/exclamatory particle here ("why/what"). بندی is 2nd- or 3rd-person singular present/archaic form of بندن (to bind) meaning "do you bind" or "you bind." دل is the direct object "heart." اندر is the locative preposition "in/within," and سرای سپنج is a poetic/metaphorical noun phrase "house/abode of sponge" (سرای = house; سپنج = sponge), i.e., a fragile or porous dwelling; note سرای here is archaic and is acting as "house/abode."
segments.s238-g32: که دانا نداند یکی را ز پنج — که introduces the explanatory/cause clause ("for/since/that"). دانا is the subject "the wise (one)." نداند is an archaic negative present/past form meaning "does not know" (نداند = نمی‌داند/نَدانَد). یکی را ز پنج is a reversed/older word order for "one of five" where را marks یکی as definite object and ز (from/of) introduces the group ("one out of five"). Together: "for even the wise do not know one of five."
segments.s238-g1: سپهبد (subject) + چنان کرد is a short clause 'the spahbed did/acted thus.' چنان (so/thus) modifies کرد (past tense of کردن, 'acted/did'); کو here is an archaic relative/connector introducing the subordinate clause 'when/that he saw a way' and is placed immediately before راه دید (verb-final subordinate clause: noun then verb). دید is simple past 'saw'.
segments.s238-g2: همی here is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('just/at once') modifying the following verb phrase. دست (literally 'hand') is used metonymically for 'force/strength' and is the object of کوتاه دید 'saw short' — i.e. 'saw that the force was short/scant.' ازان ('from that') links the noun رزم ('battle') to دست, specifying which battle. دید is again past 'saw/perceived.'
segments.s238-g3: چو رستم بیامد is a temporal clause 'when Rostam came' (چو = 'when'). مرا پای نیست is an emphatic existential/possessive pattern: مرا ('for me' = م + را, object/possessive marker) + پای ('foot/footing') + نیست ('is not') = 'I have no footing' or 'there is no footing for me.' Word order is subject/adverbial clause → predicate (existential negative).
segments.s238-g4: جز از ... رای نیست is a negative-except construction: جز ('except') + از رفتن ('except from going/withdrawal') + از پیش او ('from before him' = 'away from his presence', پیش used as 'in the presence of') + رای نیست ('there is no counsel/plan'). In other words, the only feasible course is withdrawal; there is no other counsel.
segments.s238-g5: بباید شدن is an impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary to become/one must (depart).' تا here functions as 'so that / toward' introducing a goal/direction, and بدان روی چین = 'to that side (China)' — b- + دان is an older/poetic locution meaning 'to that' + روی ('side/face') + چین ('China' here, a place-name). The verb/complement of motion is implicit and completed in the next segment.
segments.s238-g6: گر introduces a conditional 'if'; ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'in this manner' or 'thus' and qualifies the condition; گنجد (present/subjunctive sense here) means 'fits / can be contained' (it is used as potential: 'if anyone can find room'); کسی 'anyone' is the subject of گنجد; در زمین 'on the earth/land' is locative. Together: 'if in this way anyone can fit on the land.'
segments.s238-g7: درفشش بماندند is subject + verb: درفش ('the standard/banner') is the locative/topic and بماندند is past plural 'they remained'. و او خود برفت connects with 'and he himself departed' — او خود ('he himself') emphasizes the agent; برفت is an archaic/perfective past 'went off' (here: 'departed').
segments.s238-g8: سوی چین و ماچین is a directional phrase 'toward China and Māchin' (سوی = 'toward(s)'). خرامید is past simple 'he strutted/strode (proudly)' and takes تفت as its object or an attached name; the verb is narrative past describing his proud departure toward the two places. Note: ماچین and تفت here are place/names in context; supplied sense lists did not match those proper-name uses.

sentences.s238-s25: He sought a sign of the Turanian king's army; from every side they searched, both off the road and on the road.
sentences.s238-s26: No sign came from Afrasiab — neither on the mountain nor at sea, neither on dry land nor on the water.
sentences.s238-s9: The host everywhere raised a shout; everyone planted their spears on the hill.
sentences.s238-s10: Thus the gate and the plain became a battlefield, so that because of the slain they saw no place for a path.
sentences.s238-s11: They departed; one party begged for mercy and left, the fleeing ones went off toward the road.
sentences.s238-s12: Because of the lack of night-watch the herds, lowlands and possessions were ruined; the whole plain was bodies, without hands and manes.
sentences.s238-s17: At one time Ahriman comes to fight; at another, a wedding full of scent and color.
sentences.s238-s18: Choose harmlessness and the cup, for he says that a curse is better than praise.
sentences.s238-s19: Eat what you have and do not grieve, for the world is a sponge and we are passing over it.
sentences.s238-s20: Do not harm anyone for the sake of a dirham; refrain, as far as you can, from oppressing another.
sentences.s238-s5: The army advanced toward the other army; the earth became like a black cloud.
sentences.s238-s6: Tahmatan said aloud at that moment, (saying) that spears and bows and arrows (be ready).
sentences.s238-s7: Strive, and bring swords and maces; bring the skills down from on high.
sentences.s238-s8: The panther then twisted (turned) with its fury so that it might see its prey beside it.
sentences.s238-s21: They searched the plain for anything that was of gold and jewels and had not been wasted.
sentences.s238-s22: He dispatched everywhere, near the king, servants, horses, swords, and helmets.
sentences.s238-s23: From that he took his share, and he took up all the helmets, musk, and amber.
sentences.s238-s24: He gave the rest to the army — whatever there was in that battlefield.
sentences.s238-s13: Rostam said thus: "Killing is enough; the poison of Time belongs to another person."
sentences.s238-s14: At times he would bring a load of poison; at other times he would bring poison from opium.
sentences.s238-s15: Everyone, put on your battle garments; everyone, increase your good deeds.
sentences.s238-s16: Why do you bind the heart inside a sponge-like house, since even the wise do not know one of five?
sentences.s238-s1: The spahbed acted thus: when he saw a way, he perceived that the support in that battle was scant.
sentences.s238-s2: When Rostam arrived, I had no footing; there is no counsel except to withdraw from his presence.
sentences.s238-s3: They had to withdraw toward that side (China), if in such a way anyone could find room on the land.
sentences.s238-s4: They remained at the banner, and he himself departed; he strode off toward China and Māchin.
